signals.name: Leopold Aschenbrenner
signals.summary: No posts found in the last 7 days. Most recent public activity dates to mid-2025. His Situational Awareness LP fund has been widely covered in media (up ~270% YTD through May 2026, ~$20B AUM), but no new first-person output detected this week.
signals.connection: His AI-infrastructure investment thesis—betting on power, chips, and data-center buildout to support AGI—is directly relevant to today's AI IPO wave (Anthropic S-1, OpenAI, SpaceX) and the broader frontier AI model wave.

signals.name: Dan Wang
signals.summary: No new posts or X activity detected in the last 7 days. His website features evergreen commentary on US-China divergence and industrial policy, but nothing fresh this week.
signals.connection: His ongoing work on China's technological ambitions and US-China tech competition is highly relevant to today's geopolitics and China topic areas, especially in the context of AI model races.

signals.name: Nathan Benaich
signals.summary: No posts detected in the last 7 days. His X replies page shows no recent activity. His most recent notable output was hosting RAAIS 2026 on June 12 in London, covering frontier AI, open-ended agents, and space-based compute.
signals.connection: RAAIS 2026 themes—frontier AI, open-ended agents, space-based data centers—connect directly to today's simultaneous model wave (Gemini 3.5 Pro, Claude Mythos, Grok 5) and the Anthropic S-1 filing.

signals.name: Stanley Druckenmiller
signals.summary: No new interview or public remarks found in the last 7 days on CNBC, Bloomberg, or FT. Most recent coverage (Bloomberg, Feb 2026) focused on his ties to new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh and Treasury Secretary Bessent.
signals.connection: His close relationships with both Warsh and Bessent are acutely relevant today as Warsh holds rates and signals a possible regime change—a dynamic Bloomberg noted could reflect Druckenmiller's own macro principles permeating US monetary policy.

signals.name: Byrne Hobart
signals.latest_title: Anabasis, Memory, Height, Neo-Brandeisians, Canceled, Capacity, Compute, Realism
signals.summary: Published multiple issues of The Diff this week (June 5–6), covering topics including compute capacity, realism, and neo-Brandeisian antitrust thinking. His June 6 issue explicitly addresses compute and P/S ratios alongside automation themes.
signals.connection: His compute and capacity framing connects directly to the simultaneous frontier model launches (Gemini 3.5, Claude Mythos, Grok 5) and the AI IPO wave; neo-Brandeisian regulatory angle touches AI governance and the Trump AI executive order.

signals.name: Michael Burry
signals.summary: Burry has been posting daily to his Substack this week, covering dollar-cost averaging into falling-knife stocks, buying Fiserv (FISV) at ~$49.50, and publishing a sharp June 12 note arguing that LLMs are 'language models, not AI' and that AI capex is hitting diminishing returns before profitability.
signals.connection: His June 12 post is a direct counter-signal to today's AI IPO wave and model release frenzy, arguing AI capex is misallocated and that the market is punishing value stocks unfairly in favor of AI capital flows—a key tension in today's markets and digital finance topics.

signals.name: Ben Thompson
signals.latest_title: Hey Siri, Tell Me a Fable (week of June 8, 2026)
signals.summary: Thompson's week of June 8 roundup covered Apple finally shipping Intelligence, Anthropic's strategic positioning and its similarities to Apple, and five questions on WWDC 2026. He also recently covered Anthropic's government supply-chain risk designation and its existential implications, and published 'Europe's Final Warning' (June 12) on Europe's China trade exposure.
signals.connection: Directly connects to today's Anthropic S-1 filing, the frontier AI model wave, AI regulation and governance (government preview EO), and geopolitics (Europe-China tensions)—Thompson is one of the clearest voices mapping all of these simultaneously.

signals.name: Paul Graham
signals.latest_title: How to Earn a Billion Dollars
signals.summary: Graham published 'How to Earn a Billion Dollars' on June 14, 2026, drawing from an Oxford Union talk to lay out mechanics of building billionaire-level wealth through startups. He also reacted positively on June 16 to Bland AI's $100M Series C for automating complex phone calls.
signals.connection: The essay is directly relevant to today's historic AI IPO wave (Anthropic, OpenAI, SpaceX going public) and the creation of new AI-native companies; his Bland reaction touches the labor/workforce displacement theme as voice AI automates phone-based work.

signals.name: Benedict Evans
signals.summary: No new essays detected this week; his most recent piece was May 24 questioning OpenAI's strategic moat. His Spring 2026 macro presentation 'AI eats the world' remains his primary active output. His X account is set to approved followers only.
signals.connection: His May essay questioning OpenAI's lack of unique tech, limited engagement, and unproven distribution is highly resonant with Anthropic's S-1 filing and what it reveals about AI lab business models in the IPO wave.

signals.name: Packy McCormick
signals.summary: On June 2, McCormick promoted the out-of-stealth launch of Westmag, a US motor and actuator company he has invested in, arguing America needs a domestic motor manufacturing champion. No new Not Boring newsletter essay detected this week.
signals.connection: Westmag's pitch for American industrial manufacturing capability connects to the defense/security and energy themes in today's topics; domestic hardware reindustrialization is a subplot of the US-China competition story.

signals.name: CJ Gustafson
signals.summary: No new Mostly Metrics posts detected in the last 7 days; his Substack archive shows the last entry in July 2025. He recently appeared as a podcast guest commenting on the SpaceX IPO, OpenAI vs. Anthropic public market timing, and SaaS multiples in the current environment.
signals.connection: His commentary on SpaceX IPO pricing, OpenAI vs. Anthropic public market sequencing, and SaaS multiple compression directly maps to today's historic AI IPO wave story.

signals.name: Lenny Rachitsky
signals.summary: Rachitsky's pinned recent post (within 14 hours of today) promoted his Tony Fadell interview on product and hardware leadership. On June 4 he reacted with 'Wild' to Anthropic's claim that its engineers now ship 8x as much code per quarter as they did in 2021–2025.
signals.connection: His reaction to Anthropic's 8x developer productivity claim directly connects to today's Claude Mythos launch and the broader AI-accelerated software development theme; the labor/workforce topic is implicit in such productivity multipliers.

signals.name: Andrej Karpathy
signals.latest_title: On bespoke AI-native software and agent-native ergonomics
signals.summary: Karpathy has been posting about his vision for AI-native software—arguing that the 'app store' model feels outdated when LLM agents can improvise apps on demand, and that 99% of products still lack AI-native interfaces. He joined Anthropic's pretraining team earlier in 2026.
signals.connection: As a new Anthropic employee, his thinking on AI-native software and agent ergonomics is directly relevant to today's Claude Mythos launch, the Anthropic S-1, and the Trump AI EO requiring 30-day government previews of frontier models.

signals.name: Ilya Sutskever
signals.summary: No posts detected in the last 7 days. His most recent notable X post was February 27, 2026, praising Anthropic for not backing down in a government standoff. SSI (Safe Superintelligence Inc.) has been extremely quiet publicly.
signals.connection: SSI's silence is itself a signal amid today's noisy model wave (Gemini 3.5, Claude Mythos, Grok 5 all landing simultaneously); Sutskever's prior praise for Anthropic's regulatory resistance is relevant backdrop to the Anthropic S-1 and government AI preview EO.

signals.name: Noam Brown
signals.latest_title: On AlphaGo's effect on human Go players and AI's effect on math
signals.summary: On May 28, Brown posted that just as AlphaGo improved human Go players after its dominance, AI solving hard math problems will likely improve human mathematicians too—a constructive take on AI-human complementarity at the reasoning frontier.
signals.connection: His point about AI elevating human capability (rather than replacing it) is a counterweight to labor displacement fears in today's workforce topic; it also connects to the reasoning model wave as Grok 5, Gemini 3.5, and Claude Mythos all compete on reasoning benchmarks.

signals.name: François Chollet
signals.summary: No posts found in the last 7 days. His most recent major public appearance was a YC Lightcone podcast (March 2026) where he argued for building intelligence from first principles rather than scaling existing approaches. He co-founded NDEA and ARC Prize after leaving Google in late 2024.
signals.connection: His skepticism of pure scaling as a path to AGI is a direct intellectual counterpoint to today's simultaneous frontier model launches and the race dynamics driving the AI IPO wave; ARC-AGI benchmark results will be a key litmus test as Gemini 3.5, Claude Mythos, and Grok 5 ship.
